record_office_terminology.land_records: Recorder of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens, plats -- one office per county; in St. Louis City, the City Recorder of Deeds)
record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions; historically also called Probate Judge or Surrogate Court)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Recorder of Deeds (marriage licenses issued by county; some early marriages recorded by Circuit Clerk)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1910 (statewide vital registration; earlier records fragmentary; some counties have birth/death records from 1883; St. Louis City from 1870)

burned_county_authority: Missouri State Archives, Jefferson City -- Missouri has approximately 30 burned or partially burned counties, primarily due to Civil War guerrilla warfare (Bushwhacker-Jayhawker conflict), Order No. 11 (1863, forced depopulation of western Missouri border counties), and various courthouse fires. The Missouri State Archives holds microfilm copies of surviving records and can advise on alternative sources. Many burned county records can be partially reconstructed through BLM GLO land patents, federal census records, church records, and neighboring county records.
administrative_notes: Missouri has 114 counties plus St. Louis City (an independent city separate from St. Louis County since 1876) for 115 total FIPS jurisdictions. Missouri was part of the Louisiana Purchase (1803), became a territory in 1812, and achieved statehood in 1821 under the Missouri Compromise. French colonial heritage is strong in the Mississippi River corridor — Ste. Genevieve (c.1735) is the oldest permanent European settlement. Spanish governance (1762-1800) left land grant records. Missouri was a border state during the Civil War, experiencing severe guerrilla warfare particularly in western counties. Order No. 11 (August 1863) ordered the depopulation of Jackson, Cass, Bates, and Vernon counties and parts of St. Clair County. Missouri is a public land state — BLM General Land Office records document original land patents. Some areas have earlier French and Spanish colonial land grants that predate the GLO system. The Missouri State Archives in Jefferson City and the State Historical Society of Missouri (SHSMO) in Columbia are the two primary statewide repositories. St. Louis County Library headquarters has one of the nation's premier genealogical collections. Strong German immigration (St. Louis, Hermann, Westphalia), Irish, Czech, and Italian communities are well-documented. The Dred Scott case (1846-1857) originated in St. Louis — significant African American history throughout the state.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for genealogical records. Holds county records on microfilm (deeds, probate, marriages, court records), state government records, Confederate and Union military records, vital records (births/deaths from 1910, some earlier), naturalization records, convict registers, and colonial-era documents (French and Spanish). Online databases include death certificates (1910-1969), birth records (pre-1910), and soldiers' records database.

core_repositories.repository: State Historical Society of Missouri, Columbia
core_repositories.description: Premier repository for Missouri history and genealogy. Holdings include the most comprehensive collection of Missouri newspapers (3,000+ titles), county histories, family papers, manuscript collections, maps, and photographs. Research centers in Columbia, Kansas City, Rolla, Springfield, St. Louis, and Cape Girardeau. The newspaper collection is especially valuable for obituaries, legal notices, and community news.

core_repositories.source_name: St. Louis County Library -- Special Collections / Genealogy
core_repositories.repository: St. Louis County Library, Headquarters, Kirkwood
core_repositories.description: One of the premier genealogical collections in the United States. Holdings include microfilm of county records statewide, city directories, family files, census records, and published genealogies. The collection serves researchers for Missouri and surrounding states. Open to the public with no membership required.

Federal records for Missouri and surrounding states. Holdings include federal court records, naturalization records, Bureau of Indian Affairs records (Osage, Shawnee, Delaware agencies), military service records, Freedmen's Bureau records, BLM land records, and federal census records. Essential for Native American research, immigration, and federal court cases.

core_repositories.repository: Missouri Historical Society, St. Louis (Forest Park)
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository in St. Louis. Holdings include fur trade records, steamboat records, family papers, business records, photographs, and maps. Particularly strong for St. Louis metropolitan area research. Westward expansion and Lewis and Clark materials. African American history collections.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Missouri county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Missouri county courthouses and the Missouri State Archives. Key collections include Missouri marriages (1805-2002), death certificates, and county deed records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, and strategies for researching in Missouri. Covers burned counties, unique record types, and colonial-era research approaches.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Missouri newspapers. Includes St. Louis, Kansas City, Springfield, and county weeklies. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, and community news.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: State Historical Society of Missouri, Columbia
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: SHSMO holds the most comprehensive collection of Missouri newspapers — over 3,000 titles. Some are digitized and searchable online. County weeklies are essential for rural genealogy. Obituaries, marriage announcements, estate settlements, and legal notices.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Missouri Enslaved Persons and African American Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Missouri State Archives, Jefferson City / NARA-KC
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Missouri was a slave state until 1865 (Dred Scott case originated in St. Louis). Records include slave schedules (1850, 1860), estate inventories listing enslaved persons, bills of sale, freedom suits filed in St. Louis Circuit Court (digitized by Washington University), manumission records, Freedmen's Bureau records (NARA M1903, M1908), and African American church records. The 'Little Dixie' region (Boone, Callaway, Cooper, Howard, Lafayette, Saline, Randolph, etc.) had the highest concentrations of enslaved persons.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: St. Louis Freedom Suits -- Washington University Digital Gateway
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Digitized freedom suit records from St. Louis Circuit Court. Over 300 freedom suits filed by enslaved persons seeking their freedom, including the Dred Scott case. Extraordinary resource for African American genealogy in Missouri.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of the Osage, Missouri, Shawnee, Delaware, Sauk, and Fox nations in Missouri. Includes Indian agency records, treaty records (Platte Purchase 1836, Osage cessions), removal records, trade ledgers, and annuity rolls. The Osage Nation occupied much of southern and western Missouri until their removal. Trail of Tears crossings occurred in Cape Girardeau area.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Missouri State Archives / NARA-KC / St. Louis Circuit Court
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Missouri, especially St. Louis, was a major immigration gateway. Naturalization records from Missouri circuit courts at Missouri State Archives. Federal court naturalizations at NARA-KC. St. Louis attracted German, Irish, Italian, Czech (Bohemian), Polish, and other immigrant communities. St. Louis was the largest inland port in the 19th century. Ship passenger lists for Mississippi River arrivals. German Settlement Society records.

military_records.source_name: Missouri Military Records -- State Archives / NARA
military_records.repository: Missouri State Archives, Jefferson City / NARA
military_records.description: Missouri contributed soldiers to both Union and Confederate forces during the Civil War. State Archives holds Confederate service records, Union service records, militia records, loyalty oaths, and provost marshal records. Also War of 1812 militia, Indian Wars service, and Spanish-American War records. NARA holds federal military records. National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) in St. Louis holds 20th-century military personnel records.
Searchable database of Missouri military service records from the War of 1812 through World War I. Includes name, unit, rank, and service dates.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Missouri
land_and_property.description: Missouri is a public land state — original land patents documenting the first transfer of land from the federal government are searchable and downloadable. Missouri GLO records date from approximately 1818 (after Louisiana Purchase surveys). Some areas have earlier French and Spanish colonial land grants that predate the GLO system.

land_and_property.source_name: French and Spanish Colonial Land Grants -- Missouri State Archives
land_and_property.description: Colonial-era land grants from French (pre-1762, 1800-1803) and Spanish (1762-1800) governance. Particularly important for the Mississippi River corridor counties (Ste. Genevieve, St. Louis, Cape Girardeau, New Madrid). These predate BLM GLO records.

vital_records.source_name: Missouri Vital Records -- Department of Health and Senior Services
vital_records.repository: Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services / Missouri State Archives
vital_records.description: Statewide vital registration began in 1910. Some county-level records exist from 1883. St. Louis City has records from 1870. Missouri State Archives holds historical death certificates (1910-1969, searchable online) and pre-1910 county birth/death records. Modern records from Department of Health and Senior Services.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Missouri State Archives / Concordia Historical Institute / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-civil registration Missouri research. Catholic parish records from the 1750s (Ste. Genevieve, St. Louis). German Lutheran and Evangelical records from the 1830s — Concordia Historical Institute (Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod) in St. Louis is a key repository. Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian records statewide. Many German-language church records require translation. SHSMO holds some church record collections.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Missouri. Growing coverage across all 115 jurisdictions. Particularly valuable for burned counties where other records are lost. Missouri has many rural family cemeteries that are being documented.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical association coordinating local societies and publishing the Missouri State Genealogical Association Journal. Links to county-level societies.

One of the largest and most active genealogical societies in the Midwest. Publishes quarterly journal, maintains indexes, and hosts research events. Collections focus on St. Louis City and County but include statewide materials.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all Missouri counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Missouri has had several extinct or renamed counties: Lillard County (renamed Lafayette, 1825); Rives County (renamed Henry, 1841); Kinderhook County (briefly renamed Camden, 1843); Van Buren County (briefly existed 1835-1855, absorbed into Cass); and several early counties reorganized during territorial period. The Platte Purchase (1836) added new territory in northwest Missouri from which Andrew, Atchison, Buchanan, Holt, Nodaway, and Platte counties were formed. Old county names occasionally appear in deeds and other records.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Missouri has one of the highest rates of burned counties in the United States, primarily due to Civil War guerrilla warfare (Bushwhacker-Jayhawker conflict) in western and southern Missouri. Order No. 11 (August 25, 1863) ordered the depopulation of Jackson, Cass, Bates, and Vernon counties (and parts of St. Clair County), resulting in the destruction of courthouses and records. Additional courthouse fires occurred in Ozark counties through the late 1800s. The Missouri State Archives in Jefferson City holds microfilm of surviving records and advises on reconstruction strategies using BLM GLO records, federal census, church records, and neighboring county records.

burned_jurisdictions.record_losses: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned 1860; records largely destroyed by guerrilla warfare
Order No. 11 (1863) forced depopulation; courthouse burned; most pre-1863 records destroyed
Order No. 11 (1863) devastation; courthouse burned; most pre-war records destroyed
1852 courthouse fire; 1881 fire; most early records destroyed
1862 fire during Civil War; courthouse burned; significant record losses; Order No. 11 affected rural areas
1863 Battle of Carthage and guerrilla warfare; courthouse burned; pre-war records largely destroyed
Civil War destruction and 1868 fire; nearly all early records lost
1872 fire destroyed courthouse; most pre-1872 records lost
Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost
1863 courthouse burned during Civil War; 1938 courthouse fire destroyed additional records
Order No. 11 (1863) and Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records destroyed
Civil War guerrilla destruction; courthouse burned 1865; most early records lost
Order No. 11 (1863) devastation; courthouse burned; most pre-war records destroyed
1880 courthouse fire destroyed many records; 1958 fire caused additional losses
